figures.caption: Table 4. Benchmark scores (%) on the HS3-Bench test data for models that are trained on pRGB projections or use RGB-pretrained backbones (HSI-Adapter). Our HSI-only cross-domain model, HyperSL-RU-Net is listed for comparison. Best results per dataset are bold, second best are underscored.

Fig. 1. The typical approach for HSI semantic segmentation uses in-domain-inmodality training (a), i. e. training models on data from the target domain (red) and target modality (blue), but many applications face limited training data availability. An established method to address this problem is cross-modality knowledge transfer (b), bridging RGB and HSI to exploit vision foundation models. These methods either sacrifice spectral information or increase architectural complexity. As an alternative, we introduce cross-domain knowledge transfer (c), i. e. exploiting HSI foundation models from a different domain – eliminating the need to bridge modality gaps. We demonstrate the effectiveness of cross-domain approaches by systematic evaluation of all three strategies on the HS3-Bench benchmark.
Fig. 2. HyperSL-RU-Net consists of a HyperSL [9] backbone and an RU-Net [18] encoder-decoder module for semantic segmentation.
Fig. 3. HyperSL-FC consists of a HyperSL [9] backbone and and a fully connected layer followed by softmax activation for spectral classification.

Table 3. Benchmark scores (%) on the HS3-Bench [18] test data for HSI-only spectral classification models, i. e. hyperspectral data as input and hyperspectral backbone models. Best results are bold, second-best are underlined.
Table 2. Benchmark scores (%) on the HS3-Bench [18] test data for HSI-only semantic segmentation models, i. e. hyperspectral data as input and hyperspectral backbone models. Best results are bold.
